title: What is a test in Go
instruction:
A test is just a function that checks whether your code does what it should. Think of it as a quality inspector on a factory line — run the machine, check the output, flag problems.

In Go, tests follow one rule: `func TestXxx(t *testing.T)`. That's it. No test framework, no annotations, no magic.

Run this and see a passing test.
successMessage: The test passed — `countBooks()` returned 2 as expected. In a real project, `go test` runs all tests in the package automatically.

title: t.Errorf — report a failure
instruction:
`t.Errorf` marks the test as failed and logs a message — but the test keeps running.

The message format is a convention: `"functionName(inputs) = actual, want expected"`

Add a second test case: create an empty library and verify `countBooks()` equals `0`. If it does not, use `t.Errorf` with the same format.
hint: lib := Library{}  got := lib.countBooks()  if got != 0 { t.Errorf("countBooks() = %d, want 0", got) }

codeChecks.message: Call countBooks() on an empty library and capture the result.
Use t.Errorf to report if the result is wrong.
Replace the TODO comment with your test case.

successMessage: Two cases, one test function. Both pass. t.Errorf is how you say "this is wrong" without stopping the whole test — other cases still run and you see all failures at once.

title: t.Fatal — stop on first failure
instruction:
Sometimes a failure makes the rest of the test pointless. Use `t.Fatalf` to stop immediately.

For example: if the library is nil, there is no point trying to count books.

Run this. Notice that when `books` is nil, `t.Fatalf` stops — the second check never runs. Then change `nil` to an empty slice and see both checks pass.
hint: Change books: nil to books: []Book{} in main's test call.
successMessage: t.Fatalf stops the test immediately — useful when a failure makes all subsequent assertions meaningless. t.Errorf continues — useful when you want to see all failures at once.

title: Table-driven tests
hint: Add {"000-000", nil} and {"978-789", &Book{"Testing In Go", "978-789"}} to the tests slice.

codeChecks.message: Add the not-found case: {"000-000", nil}
Add the found case for ISBN 978-789.
Replace the TODO comment with your two test cases.

successMessage: 4/4 passed. Table-driven tests are the gold standard in Go. Add a new case to the table, the loop handles it. No duplication, no copy-paste.

title: Testing error cases
instruction:
Your tests should cover failure paths too — not just the happy path.

For a function like `lookupByISBN`, test both:
1. When the book exists — verify a non-nil book is returned with the right ISBN
2. When the book does not exist — verify `nil` is returned

Add a test case for searching `"999-999"` which should return `nil`.
hint: {isbn: "999-999", want: nil}  — in the loop: if tt.want == nil { if got != nil { t.Errorf(...) } } else { if got == nil || got.ISBN != tt.want.ISBN { t.Errorf(...) } }

codeChecks.message: Add the not-found case: {"999-999", nil}
Replace the TODO comment with the error test case.

successMessage: 3/3 passed — including the not-found case. Always test failure paths. The bugs that hurt most are the ones in code nobody thought to test.

title: t.Run — subtests with names

codeChecks.message: Wrap each test case in t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T) { ... }).
Use fmt.Sprintf to create the test name from tt.isbn.
Replace the TODO comment with your t.Run call.

successMessage: Named subtests. When this fails in a real project, Go reports exactly which subtest failed: TestLookupByISBN/lookupByISBN(999-999). No guessing which input broke.

title: Testing with interfaces — mocking
instruction:
Real code often depends on external things: databases, APIs, ISBN lookup services. You cannot call those in tests.

The solution: define the dependency as an interface. In tests, swap in a fake implementation.

Run this. `ISBNService` is an interface. `mockService` is a fake that returns a canned book instead of calling a real API. The test verifies `LookupBook` calls `FetchByISBN` with the right ISBN — no real lookup made.
successMessage: No real ISBN lookup made. The mock recorded the call, the test verified the behaviour. This pattern — interface + fake implementation — is how all professional Go testing works.

title: Benchmarks
successMessage: Both find the book but the map lookup is significantly faster for large libraries — O(1) vs O(n). Benchmarks reveal these differences.

title: Test helpers with t.Helper()
instruction:
When you have repeated assertion logic, extract it into a helper function and call `t.Helper()` inside it.

This tells Go: if this helper fails, report the line in the calling test — not the line inside the helper. Much easier to debug.

Run this. The `assertFound` helper is used three times. Then add a fourth call that checks searching for `"978-789"` returns a book titled `"Network Programming"`.

codeChecks.message: Add: assertFound(t, lib, "978-789", "Network Programming")
Replace the TODO comment with your assertion.

successMessage: t.Helper() is a small thing that makes debugging much easier. Without it, every failure points to a line inside assertFound. With it, Go points to the exact test line that failed.

title: Test a real function — searchCatalog
instruction:
Write tests for a function you define yourself.

`searchCatalog(query string) []Book` searches the library's catalog for books whose title contains the query (case-insensitive).

`"go"` → finds `["Go 101", "Network Programming in Go"]`
`"python"` → finds `[]`
`"network"` → finds `["Network Programming in Go"]`

First implement `searchCatalog`. Then write a table-driven test with at least 5 cases.
hint: strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(b.Title), strings.ToLower(query)) — iterate over l.Books, collect matches.

codeChecks.message: Implement the searchCatalog function.
Use strings.Contains and strings.ToLower for case-insensitive matching.
Add at least 5 test cases with expected result counts.
Replace both TODO comments with your implementation and test cases.

successMessage: You implemented and tested a search function from scratch. The test is the specification. If someone breaks searchCatalog later, your tests catch it immediately.

title: Fix the failing test
instruction:
The test exists. The function is broken.

`lookupByISBN(isbn string) *Book` should return the book with the matching ISBN, or `nil` if not found.

But the implementation is wrong. Fix `lookupByISBN` so all test cases pass.
hint: for _, b := range l.Books { if b.ISBN == isbn { return &b } }  return nil

codeChecks.message: Compare b.ISBN == isbn, not b.Title == isbn.
Return nil if no book is found.

successMessage: 4/4 passed. The test was right — the function was wrong. That is the value of tests: they are the objective referee. Fix the code, run the tests, trust the result.

title: Build a tested library catalog
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a library catalog with full test coverage:

1. Define `Book` and `Library` types
2. Implement three functions: `addBook(title, isbn string)`, `findByISBN(isbn string) *Book`, `listBooks() []Book`
3. Write a `TestCatalog` function with a table-driven test covering:
   - Adding books to the library
   - Finding books by ISBN (both found and not-found cases)
   - Listing all books
4. Use `t.Run` to name each subtest
5. Print the pass count at the end

Expected output ends with:
```
8/8 cases passed
```
(or more, depending on how many cases you add)
hint: Define a Library struct with a Books slice and methods. Use a table with op string field to distinguish operations.

codeChecks.message: Implement the addBook method.
Implement the findByISBN method.
Implement the listBooks method.
Use t.Run to name each subtest.
Include a not-found test case where findByISBN returns nil.
Print the pass count at the end.

successMessage: A fully tested library catalog. Three methods, eight-plus test cases, named subtests, coverage for found and not-found cases. This is production-quality Go code. You now test like a professional.
